$comment: Module 7 - Quantum Communication & Cryptography. One item per objective. Grading matches apps.quizzes.views.
title: Module 7 - Quantum Communication & Cryptography

questions.text: Quantum teleportation transfers an unknown state using:
questions.explanation: Teleportation consumes one shared Bell pair (ebit) and two classical bits to move an unknown qubit state — no qubit is physically transmitted.

options.text: a pre-shared Bell pair and two classical bits
a faster-than-light quantum channel
a copy of the unknown state
only classical communication, no entanglement

questions.text: The correct order of the teleportation protocol is:
questions.explanation: Alice performs a Bell-basis measurement on the unknown qubit and her half of the pair, sends the two outcome bits, and Bob applies the indicated Pauli ($I,X,Z,ZX$) to recover the state.

options.text: Bell measurement on the unknown qubit + Alice's half → send 2 classical bits → Bob applies a Pauli correction
Bob measures first, then Alice sends her qubit
Alice clones the qubit and mails it
a joint measurement of all three qubits at the end only

questions.text: Why does teleportation violate neither no-cloning nor causality?
questions.hint: One copy ever exists; classical bits gate the result.
questions.explanation: The Bell measurement destroys Alice's copy, so only one $|\psi\rangle$ exists; and without the two classical bits Bob's half is maximally mixed, so no information moves faster than light.

options.text: Alice's measurement destroys the original (no clone), and Bob's qubit is useless until the classical bits arrive (no faster-than-light signal)
it does violate no-cloning but not causality
the classical bits travel faster than light
two copies of the state briefly exist, which is allowed

questions.text: How many classical bits must be communicated to teleport one qubit?
questions.explanation: Exactly 2 classical bits (the Bell-measurement result) are needed, alongside one shared ebit, to teleport a single qubit.

quizzes.description: Two bits per qubit, Bell-state encoding, duality, resources.

questions.hint: Entanglement doubles the classical capacity of the qubit.
questions.explanation: With shared entanglement, transmitting a single qubit conveys two classical bits — the dual of teleportation.

options.text: two classical bits by transmitting one qubit, using a pre-shared Bell pair
one classical bit using two qubits
an unknown quantum state using two classical bits
two qubits using one classical bit

questions.text: How does Alice encode her two bits?
questions.hint: The four Paulis map $|\Phi^+\rangle$ to the four Bell states.
questions.explanation: Applying $I,X,Z$, or $ZX$ to one half of $|\Phi^+\rangle$ produces one of the four mutually orthogonal Bell states, which Bob distinguishes with a Bell measurement.

options.text: she applies one of $\{I, X, Z, ZX\}$ to her half, mapping the shared pair to one of the four orthogonal Bell states
she measures her half in the computational basis
she clones her qubit twice
she applies a random unitary Bob cannot invert

questions.text: Superdense coding is best described as:
questions.hint: Teleportation: 1 ebit + 2 cbits → 1 qubit; superdense: 1 ebit + 1 qubit → 2 cbits.
questions.explanation: The two protocols are duals: teleportation spends entanglement + classical bits to send a qubit; superdense coding spends entanglement + a qubit to send classical bits.

options.text: the dual of teleportation — trading the roles of the quantum and classical channels
identical to teleportation in every respect
a form of quantum error correction
unrelated to entanglement

questions.text: The resource ledger for superdense coding is:
questions.explanation: One shared ebit plus one physically sent qubit deliver two classical bits — without the shared entanglement a single qubit carries only one bit (Holevo).

options.text: one ebit + one transmitted qubit convey two classical bits
two ebits convey one classical bit
one qubit alone conveys two classical bits with no entanglement
one ebit conveys four classical bits

quizzes.description: Conjugate bases, sifting, eavesdropping detection, security assumptions.

questions.text: BB84 establishes a shared secret key by:
questions.hint: Two mutually unbiased bases underpin the protocol.
questions.explanation: Alice encodes random bits in a random basis ($\{|0\rangle,|1\rangle\}$ or $\{|+\rangle,|-\rangle\}$); after transmission the parties publicly compare bases (not values) to build a shared key.

options.text: encoding random bits in randomly chosen conjugate bases ($Z$ or $X$) and comparing bases publicly
sending the key in plaintext over a classical channel
cloning qubits to distribute copies of the key
factoring a large number the eavesdropper cannot

questions.text: During basis reconciliation (sifting), Alice and Bob:
questions.hint: Matched bases give correlated bits; mismatched are random.
questions.explanation: They announce their bases and retain the ~50% of rounds where the bases agreed (the sifted key); mismatched-basis rounds are uncorrelated and discarded.

options.text: keep only the bits where their randomly chosen bases matched and discard the rest
keep every bit regardless of basis
reveal all the bit values publicly
keep only the bits where the bases differed

questions.text: How is an eavesdropper detected in BB84?
questions.explanation: Because Eve cannot clone the unknown states, intercept-resend forces her to guess the basis; wrong guesses disturb the states, elevating the QBER above the noise floor and revealing her.

options.text: unable to clone the qubits, Eve must measure and guess the basis, disturbing states and raising the quantum bit-error rate (QBER)
Eve's presence changes the classical bits in transit
the protocol slows down measurably
Eve cannot be detected at all

questions.text: Which assumption is essential for BB84's security?
questions.hint: QKD gives key exchange, not authentication.
questions.explanation: BB84 is information-theoretically secure only with an authenticated classical channel; without authentication an active attacker can impersonate the parties. QKD provides a key, which then feeds a classical cipher.

options.text: the classical channel must be authenticated, or a man-in-the-middle can defeat the protocol
the quantum channel must be lossless
Alice and Bob must share a prior secret as long as the message
the eavesdropper must obey classical physics only

quizzes.description: Photon loss, entanglement swapping, repeaters, outlook.

questions.text: Why is direct long-distance quantum communication so hard?
questions.explanation: Fiber attenuation makes the transmission probability drop exponentially with length, and the no-cloning theorem rules out classical-style signal amplification.

options.text: photon survival in fiber falls exponentially with distance, and no-cloning forbids amplifying the signal
quantum states travel slower than classical signals
fiber cannot carry single photons at all
entanglement decays only linearly with distance

questions.hint: It teleports entanglement across a node.
questions.explanation: If A-M and M-B are each entangled, a Bell measurement at M entangles A and B directly — the primitive for extending entanglement over distance.

options.text: a Bell measurement at a midpoint links two independently entangled segments so the endpoints become entangled
copies an entangled pair to make more pairs
measures the endpoints to destroy entanglement
amplifies a photon without cloning

questions.hint: Swap to extend, purify for fidelity, memory to synchronize.
questions.explanation: Repeaters chain entanglement swapping across short segments, use purification to raise fidelity, and quantum memories to synchronize the probabilistic steps — beating direct-transmission loss.

options.text: entanglement swapping, entanglement purification, and quantum memory
a laser amplifier and a classical repeater only
cloning and measurement
a single long fiber with no intermediate nodes

questions.text: What is the honest status of the quantum internet?
questions.hint: Early experiments exist; large-scale networks do not yet.
questions.explanation: Metropolitan QKD and small experimental networks are real today, but a large-scale, memory-based quantum internet remains an active long-term research goal.

options.text: small networks and metropolitan QKD links exist; a full memory-based quantum internet is a long-term goal
a global quantum internet is already deployed
it is physically impossible in principle
it requires no quantum memory or repeaters
